slots.text: Most founders treat user interviews like interrogations. You're not trying to extract confession of need. You're trying to understand their Tuesday afternoon.

Signs your idea validation is actually just confirmation bias:

1. You only talk to people who already agree with you
2. You're asking leading questions
3. You ignore every 'no' as 'they don't get it'
4. You haven't talked to 20+ strangers yet

The AI tooling paradox: everyone wants to automate their workflows, but most workflows are broken to begin with. You end up with really efficient terrible processes. Fix the process first, then automate.
Founders who build in public get better feedback than founders who build in stealth. Not because the audience is smarter, but because explaining forces clarity.
Removing friction sounds simple until you realize most friction exists for a reason. The art is knowing which friction protects you and which friction kills you.
The hardest part of talking to users isn't finding them or getting them to talk. It's sitting with the silence after you ask a question and not filling it with your own answer.
Most founders ask users what they want instead of watching what they actually do. The gap between those two things is where most products die.

Three things that kill idea validation:

1. Only talking to people who already like you
2. Asking hypothetical questions instead of real behavior
3. Confusing politeness for product-market fit
The founders who validate ideas fastest are the ones who get comfortable with users saying no. They treat rejection as data, not failure.
Building with AI feels like having a really smart intern who confidently makes stuff up. You can move fast but you're constantly fact-checking. The productivity gains are real but so is the paranoia.
Friction in workflows compounds worse than interest. One extra click becomes ten extra minutes becomes avoiding the tool entirely.

Signs your workflow has too much friction:

1. You procrastinate using your own tools
2. You create workarounds for workarounds
3. You find excuses to do things manually
4. Your team stops logging data
Most founders think idea validation means asking "would you use this?" Wrong question. Ask "how do you solve this problem today?" then listen for the workarounds and frustration in their voice.

Signs you're talking to the wrong users:
1. They love everything you show them
2. They ask when it'll be "totally free"
3. They suggest 47 new features
4. They've never paid for software before
5. They say "this would be nice to have"

Building with AI tools is weird. You can prototype faster than ever but you also ship half-baked ideas faster than ever. The bottleneck isn't coding anymore—it's knowing what actually matters to users.

Founders who obsess over removing friction from their own workflows but ignore friction in their user research process. You'll spend 3 hours automating a 5-minute task but won't schedule proper user interviews.
The best user feedback comes from people who are already solving your problem with duct tape and spreadsheets. Find the duct tape users.

What actually happens when you remove workflow friction:
1. People do more of that thing
2. They notice other friction points
3. They expect everything else to be frictionless too
4. You created a new standard to meet
Most founders think validation means asking "would you buy this?" Wrong question. Ask "what's the last time you tried to solve this problem?" and watch their energy change completely.

Three signs you're talking to the wrong users:

1. They love everything you show them
2. They ask when it'll be ready but won't commit to testing
3. They keep suggesting features for "other people like them"

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the tech stack. It's that users expect magic but can't articulate what magic looks like. You end up building impressive demos that solve yesterday's problems.

Founders who obsess over removing friction from their own workflows ship faster than founders who outsource everything. Strange how building tools for yourself creates better intuition for building tools for others.
User interviews where people describe their current workarounds are worth 10x more than interviews where they describe their dream solution. Workarounds reveal actual behavior.

Workflow friction usually hides in these places:

1. The handoff between tools
2. The 3rd step of a 5-step process
3. The thing people do "just to be safe"
4. Whatever happens when the happy path breaks
Most founders treat user interviews like surveys with voices. They ask what features people want instead of understanding what jobs they're trying to get done.

Three things that kill idea validation:

1. Asking hypothetical questions
2. Talking to friends who lie to be nice
3. Building before you understand the problem

All avoidable. Most people do all three.

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the tech. It's that everything feels possible, so you build features users don't actually need. The constraints that used to guide product decisions are gone.
Founders who remove one click from their signup flow see it as a win. Founders who remove one entire step from their user's day build companies.
User interviews should make you uncomfortable. If someone agrees with everything you say, you're talking to the wrong person or asking the wrong questions.

Signs you're automating the wrong workflow:

1. You spent more time building it than it saves
2. Users work around your automation
3. It breaks more than it helps
4. Nobody asked for it
Most founders skip validation because they're scared of hearing 'no.' But getting a clear 'no' is infinitely more valuable than a polite 'maybe' that wastes six months of your life.

Signs you're talking to the wrong users:
1. They love everything you show them
2. They ask zero follow-up questions
3. They won't commit to trying it
4. They suggest features for 'other people'
5. They change the subject quickly

The hardest part about building with AI isn't the tech. It's that everyone expects magic, but most valuable use cases are boring workflow improvements. You're selling efficiency to people who want to be impressed.
Founders who obsess over removing friction from their product often add massive friction to their sales process. Your landing page has 47 steps but your checkout flow is seamless.
User interviews are not market research. Stop asking what they want. Start asking what they actually do when the problem hits them right now.
Every workflow tool promises to save time, but most create new work. The real question isn't 'will this make things faster' but 'will people actually change their habits for this.'
Most founders skip user interviews because they're afraid their idea sucks. The ones who don't skip them are afraid their idea sucks too. The difference is one group finds out early.

3 things that kill validation interviews:

1. Leading questions ("Would you use X?" vs "How do you handle Y now?")
2. Talking to friends who want to be nice
3. Asking about future behavior instead of past pain

AI tools promise to automate everything but most founders use them to automate the wrong things. We're building chatbots before we know what conversations matter. Classic solution-first thinking with better tech.
Founders who say "we're removing friction" usually mean "we're adding another app to someone's workflow." Real friction removal means doing less, not doing it differently.
The best validation question isn't "Would you pay for this?" It's "What's the worst part of how you handle this today?" Pain drives behavior. Convenience suggestions don't.

Signs you're validating an idea vs validating your assumptions:

1. You change your questions based on what you learn
2. You talk to people who might hate your solution
3. You can explain why 5 people said no
